notes.note: Caelius congratulates Cicero on the betrothal of his daughter Tullia to P. Cornelius Dolabella, the dissolute young noble whose faults Caelius here charitably plays down; the match was made in 50 BC while Cicero was abroad.

notes.anchor: this business of the veto on the provinces
notes.note: The tribune Curio had been vetoing every senatorial move to recall Caesar from his provinces; the Senate now tried to override the obstruction, and its failure to do so is the victory Caelius reports.
Renders the formula in alia omnia (discessio), a vote in which the Senate divided against M. Marcellus's motion, defeating the move to act against Curio's veto.
Caelius's barbed phrase (senes divites) for the wealthy ex-consuls and elder statesmen, Cicero among them, whom he taunts for leaving the Republic's defence to others.

notes.anchor: Q. Hortensius, as I write this letter, was at his last gasp
notes.note: Q. Hortensius Hortalus, the great orator and Cicero's lifelong rival at the bar, was dying as Caelius wrote (mid-50 BC).
